rulings.Case Title: CHARLES SCHWAB & CO INC. VS. LINDA T CHEN
rulings.Calendar Matter: Petitioner Charles Schwab Co., Inc.S Notice Of Motion And Motion To File Redacted Exhibits To The Reply Declaration Of Anthony L. Valenzuela, Esq.
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Monday, June 17, 2019, Line 2, PETITIONER CHARLES SCHWAB & CO INC. Petitioner Charles Schwab Co., Inc.S Notice Of Motion And Motion To File Redacted Exhibits To The Reply Declaration Of Anthony L. Valenzuela, Esq. Late Tentative. Petitioner's motion to file redacted exhibits to the Reply Declaration of Anthony L. Valenzuela, Esq. is granted. No opposition filed and the motion complies with California Rules of Court, Rules 1.201, 2.550 and 2.551. The Court finds that there exists an overriding interest that overcomes the right of public access to the portions of the records to be sealed; the overriding interest supports sealing those records; a substantial probability exists that the overriding interest will be prejudiced if the record is not sealed; the proposed sealing is narrowly tailored; and no less restrictive means exist to achieve the overriding interest. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the Petitioner is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: CHARLES SCHWAB & CO INC. VS. LINDA T CHEN
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Hearing On Petition And Motion To Confirm Finra Dr Arbitration Award As To Respondent Linda T Chen
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Monday, June 17, 2019, Line 1, PETITIONER CHARLES SCHWAB & CO INC.'S Notice Of Hearing On Petition And Motion To Confirm Finra Dr Arbitration Award As To Respondent Linda T Chen Petitioner Charles Schwab & Co., Inc.'s petition and motion to confirm FINRA-DR arbitration award as to Respondent Linda T. Chen is granted. Contractual arbitration awards are subject to judicial review only on narrowly limited grounds. Respondent has not shown that any of the statutory grounds for vacating an arbitration award apply. (Code Civ. Proc. § 1286.2.) The only ground Respondent raises is Code of Civil Procedure section 1286.2(a)(5), which states that the court shall vacate the award if "[t]he rights of the party were substantially prejudiced by the refusal of the arbitrators to postpone the hearing upon sufficient cause being shown therefor or by the refusal of the arbitrators to hear evidence material to the controversy or by other conduct of the arbitrators contrary to the provisions of this title." However, Respondent did not request the panel to postpone the hearing, and has not shown that the arbitrators' conduct violated any provision of the California Arbitration Act. In any event, Respondent's assertion that she "was never served with the Statement of Claim prior to the hearing on the petition" is not supported by the record, which shows that on May 1, 2018, she was served with the Statement of Claim by FINRA at her home (and account) address, which constituted proper service under the parties' arbitration agreement. Respondent's assertion that she lacked capacity to defend against the arbitration is unsupported by a sufficient evidentiary showing. In any event, Respondent has not shown that she had any defense on the merits to Schwab's claim, and therefore cannot show that she suffered substantial prejudice. (See Swab Financial v. E*Trade Securities (2007) 150 Cal.App.4th 1181, 1198 ["if there was an abuse of discretion, the trial court must determine whether the moving party suffered substantial prejudice as a result"]; Hall v. Superior Court (1993) 18 Cal.App.4th 427, 438-439 [trial court erred in vacating arbitration award where moving party failed to show substantial prejudice].) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The court will sign the proposed judgment confirming the arbitration award included with the moving papers. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: KEVIN NEALAN VS. LEDCOR CONSTRUCTION, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Leave To Amend Answer
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Monday, June 17, 2019, Line 4, DEFENDANT LEDCOR CONSTRUCTION, INC. Motion For Leave To Amend Answer Defendant and Cross-Complainant Ledcor Construction Inc.'s motion for leave to amend answer is granted. "Leave to amend a pleading, including an answer, is entrusted to the sound discretion of the trial court." (Hong Sang Market, Inc. v. Peng (2018) 20 Cal.App.5th 474, 488 [no abuse of discretion in allowing amendment to add res judicata defense].) Plaintiff has not shown that Ledcor unduly delayed in seeking leave to amend, or that he would be prejudiced if the amendment is allowed. (See id. [plaintiff failed to show prejudice in defendant's delay in seeking leave to amend, where issue was purely legal in nature and it was unclear what discovery plaintiff might have conducted that would bear upon the new defense].) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for defendant is required to prepare a proposed order which states that the motion was not opposed and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: STEVEN M. WEBER VS. CUSTOM SCAFFOLDING, INC ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Further Responses Of Cross Defendant Bay Point Control Inc Dba Marina Mechanical To Request For Production Of Documents Set One
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, June 18, 2019, Line 3, DEFENDANT CUSTOM SCAFFOLD's Motion To Compel Further Responses Of Cross Defendant Bay Point Control Inc Dba Marina Mechanical To Request For Production Of Documents Set One. Pro Tem Judge Scott Borrowman, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Motion to compel GRANTED. No timely opposition filed. RFP 1: The privacy objection is overruled. In Williams v. Sup. Ct. (2017) 3 Cal.5th 531, 554-55, the Supreme Court permitted discovery of some personnel information of non-parties based on certain showings. Here, the RFP seeks the records of Plaintiff, who is a party to this lawsuit and could either request the documents in discovery himself or seek a protective order under CCP 2031.060 if he objects to the RFP. The parties may meet-and-confer about a protective order or other stipulation that will limit use of the personnel records to the handling of this case. RFP 3: The RFP permissibly requested production of native format electronic files. CCP 2031.030.(a)(2). Marina Mechanical's response did not object to the requested form of production. CCP 2031.210(d). Marina Mechanical is ordered to produce complete electronically stored information, with metadata intact, within 10 days of the hearing. RFP 5: To the extent the response to this RFP seeks personnel records or is based on the privacy objection, the Court adopts here its ruling on RFP 1. To the extent the this RFP relates to electronic documents, the Court adopts here its ruling on RFP 3 regarding form of production. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to sborrowman@gmail.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: HONG HAO WANG VS. ALICE TOY
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant Kevin Chen'S Motion For Protective Order Limiting Discovery
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, June 18, 2019, Line 7, Defendant Kevin Chen'S Motion For Protective Order Limiting Discovery. Pro Tem Judge Scott Borrowman, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: The motion for a protective order is DENIED. The subpoenaed records are "bank statements and cancelled check images" from a specific bank account. Based on Chen's deposition testimony, the account was used for business purposes. Defendant did not object to production of records from this account for a different time period. Defendant does not show how "bank statements and cancelled check images" would contain personnel or other private records of third parties that may be subject to privacy protection beyond that enjoyed by Defendants. As such, it is not clear that the constitutional right of privacy applies. See SCC Acquisitions Inc. v Sup. Ct. (2015) 243 Cal.App.4th 741, 755-56 (finding that corporations do not have a constitutional right to privacy but a lesser right to privacy). The subpoenaed records may show whether Chen, or others with authority for the account, were associated with business related to Plaintiff's claims, including revenue and expenses such as rent associated with the subject premises, and/or whether Chen continued to have business revenue or expenses associated with the premises after he claimed to have left the property. Therefore, the records are discoverable. CCP 2017.010. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to sborrowman@gmail.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: LORENZO LOPEZ VS. ROWSEY ENTERPRISES, LLC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Change Venue
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Monday, June 17, 2019, Line 6, DEFENDANT ROWSEY INVESTMENTS, LLC., ROWSEY ENTERPRISES, LLC, ROWSEY ENTERPRISES, LLC, DBA RENEGADE CLASSICS Motion To Change Venue Defendants Rowsey Enterprises, LLC, dba Renegade Classics, Rowsey Enterprises, LLC, and Rowsey Investments, LLC's motion to change venue, belatedly joined by Defendant Roadhouse Distribution, Inc. f/k/a/ Voss Distribution, Inc., is denied. Defendants have not met their burden to show that "the convenience of witnesses and the ends of justice would be promoted by the change." (Code Civ. Proc. § 397(c).) "A much more extensive factual showing is required for motions based on the 'convenience of witnesses and the ends of justice.'" (California Practice Guide: Civil Procedure Before Trial (The Rutter Group 2018) 3:576.) "Such a motion must, of course, be supported by competent evidence." (Tutor-Saliba-Perini Joint Venture v. Superior Court (1991) 233 Cal.App.3d 736, 744; Lieppman v. Lieber (1986) 180 Cal.App.3d 914, 919.) Here, Defendants' motion is devoid of any supporting evidence regarding the identity and residence of the witnesses, the reasons it would be inconvenient for them to appear locally, or the reasons why the ends of justice would be promoted by transfer to a different county. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for plaintiffs is required to prepare a proposed order which states that the motion was not opposed and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: MOAB INVESTMENT GROUP, LLC VS. COURTHOUSE VENTURES, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Protective Order
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, June 18, 2019, Line 5, DEFENDANT COURTHOUSE VENTURES, INC., MAXIMO INVESTMENTS, LLC, MOHAMMED REZAIAN, ZAHRA REZAIAN, FAEZEH REZAIAN, MOHAMMED HADIPOUR's Motion For Protective Order. Pro Tem Judge Scott Borrowman, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: The motion for protective order is DENIED. Plaintiff served discovery requests by its attorney of record, Mr. Sperandio. From the docket it appears that the Court appointed a receiver for Plaintiff, though the powers of that receiver are the subject of other motions. It also appears Mr. Sperandio has moved to be permitted to withdraw. I decline any invitation to prejudge the motion to withdraw, the motions about the receiver and his powers, or other relief the Court may impose to resolve the conflict among the partners. See Jarvis v. Jarvis (2019), 33 Cal. App. 5th 113. Limiting the analysis to the motion for protective order under CCP 2017.020 and 2031.060 (the bases cited in the notice), Defendants fail to support the motion with anything more than generalized objections about undue burden. This is insufficient. The requests for sanctions are denied. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to sborrowman@gmail.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: MARK R ROBERTS VS. CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, June 18, 2019, Line 8, DEFENDANT CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO'S DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT Defendant City and County of San Francisco's demurrer to Plaintiff Mark R. Roberts' first amended complaint is sustained with leave to amend. The FAC does not state a cause of action for common counts because it does not allege that the City is indebted to Plaintiff for a specific sum or that Plaintiff provided goods or services to the City for which he was uncompensated. (See Farmers Insurance Exchange v. Zerin (1997) 53 Cal.App.4th 445, 460 [essential allegations of a common count are the statement of indebtedness in a certain, the consideration, i.e., goods sold, work done, etc., and nonpayment].) However, Plaintiff may be able to state a viable cause of action arising out of the facts alleged, the thrust of which is that the City did not hire him as a truck driver in violation of its published policies. Plaintiff is granted 20 days leave to amend. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the City is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for defendants is required to prepare a proposed judgment of dismissal which states that the motion was not opposed and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: STEPHEN WEISSBERG VS. WELLS FARGO BANK, N.A. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, June 18, 2019, Line 14, DEFENDANT WELLS FARGO BANK, N.A.'s DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT Defendant Wells Fargo's demurrer to Plaintiff Stephen Weissberg's complaint is sustained with 20 days leave to amend. Statutory causes of action must be pled with specificity. (Carter v. Prime Healthcare Paradise Valley LLC (2011) 198 Cal.App.4th 396, 410.) Plaintiff's allegations that he sent three demands for statement of condition to Wells Fargo and Defendant Peter Fazio on January 4, 2019, and that neither Defendant provided a statement of condition are not pled with sufficient specificity. Plaintiff has leave to amend to plead to which addresses he sent the demands, whether Wells Fargo and Mr. Fazio received them, and when any response was provided to Plaintiff. Whether Plaintiff complied with the requirements of Civil Code section 2943(e)(5) that "the demand shall be made to the branch or office set forth in the payment billing notice or payment book" should be made apparent in the pleading. A violation of Civil Code section 2943(e)(4) is contingent on a willful failure to prepare and deliver the statement 21 days "after receipt of the written demand." The complaint as pled omits crucial specifics about whether and when Defendants received the demands and if they willfully delayed for 21 days. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Defendant is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: STEPHEN WEISSBERG VS. WELLS FARGO BANK, N.A. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion To Reclassify The Case As Limited Jurisdiction Action
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, June 18, 2019, Line 14, DEFENDANT WELLS FARGO BANK, N.A.'s Motion To Reclassify The Case As Limited Jurisdiction Action Defendant Wells Fargo's motion to reclassify as limited jurisdiction action is granted. A court is empowered to reclassify an unlimited civil case as limited if there is no reasonable possibility that the court could render a judgment greater than $25,000. (Ytuarte v. Superior Court (2005) 129 Cal.App.4th 266, 276-277; Singer v. Superior Court (1999) 70 Cal.App.4th 1315, 1320; Walker v. Superior Court (1991) 53 Cal.3d 257, 269.) Plaintiff seeks statutory damages of $900 under Civil Code section 2943. The complaint also alleges that as a result of Defendants' refusal to provide a statement of condition, Plaintiff is unable to levy on the property "and has lost the ability to collect interest on its judgment in an amount according to proof." (Compl. 14, 18.) However, that allegation is insufficient to show that Plaintiff can show he has sustained damages in excess of the jurisdictional limit, since at most Defendants' failure to provide the required statements may have delayed Plaintiff's ability to levy on the property, and would not prelude him from recovering interest on the underlying judgment, which is continuing to accrue. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Defendant is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: TIMOTHY HANSEN ET AL VS. CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Hearing On Motion To Strike Punitive Damages And Alcohol Allegations From Plaintiffs Complaint
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, June 18, 2019, Line 15, DEFENDANT PAUL YEP's Motion To Strike Punitive Damages And Alcohol Allegations From Plaintiffs Complaint Defendant Paul Yep's motion to strike punitive damages and alcohol allegations from Plaintiffs Timothy Hansen and Erin Hansen's complaint is granted. Paragraph 6, the second sentence of paragraph 12, and the prayer for punitive damages are struck from the complaint. The allegations in the complaint do not support a prayer for punitive damages. Civil Code section 3294(a) limits punitive damages to circumstances "where it is proven by clear and convincing evidence that the defendant has been guilty of oppression, fraud, or malice." Punitive damages may not be pled with conclusory allegations; Plaintiff must plead facts to support the request. (See G.D. Searle & Co. v. Superior Court (1975) 49 Cal.App.3d 22, 29 ["Notwithstanding relaxed pleading criteria, certain tortious injuries demand firm allegations. Vague, conclusory allegations . . . may not be rescued by the rule of liberal construction."].) As explained in the Court's ruling on Defendant's demurrer, there is no basis for a cause of action for intentional infliction of emotional distress. And there are no other specifically pled facts to support a finding of oppression, fraud, or malice. The allegations in paragraphs 6 and 12 on information and belief that Defendant had been drinking and was under the influence of alcohol are conclusory and lack basis in ultimate facts. The complaint does not allege that Defendant was slurring his words, had difficulty standing, or exhibited other symptoms of alcohol consumption. Rather, it merely alleges that he caused a car accident and subsequently behaved in an evasive manner when asked to provide identification and insurance information. That is not a sufficient basis to make these allegations, nor are they sufficient to support a prayer for punitive damages. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Defendant is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portions of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: TIMOTHY HANSEN ET AL VS. CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, June 18, 2019, Line 15, DEFENDANT PAUL YEP's DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT Defendant Paul Yep's demurrer to Plaintiff Timothy Hansen's complaint is sustained without leave to amend as to the third cause of action for intentional infliction of emotional distress. A viable claim for intentional infliction of emotional distress requires "outrageous conduct" on behalf of the defendant. (Davidson v. City of Westminster (1982) 32 Cal.3d 197, 209.) For conduct to be outrageous, it must be "so extreme as to exceed all bounds of that usually tolerated in a civilized community." (Id.) "[I]t is generally held that there can be no recovery for mere profanity, obscenity, or abuse, without circumstances of aggravation, or for insults, indignities or threats which are considered to amount to nothing more than mere annoyances." (Yurick v. Superior Court (1989) 209 Cal.App.3d 1116, 1128.) Defendant's conduct as pled does not constitute outrageous conduct as a matter of law. Even liberally construing the complaint, Defendant's alleged evasiveness, his statement that he is a police officer while throwing down his identification, and driving past the Hansen household while staring in a "menacing fashion," are annoyances, but do not rise to the level of extreme and intolerable behavior. (See id.; Davidson, 32 Cal.3d at 209; Compl. 5, 26-31.) Molko v. Holy Spirit Assn. (1988) 46 Cal.3d 1092, 1102-1106, 1122 involved very different circumstances of a church abusing its authority by fraudulently coercing the plaintiffs into joining and serving the church. By contrast, Defendant's statements and behavior here, while allegedly unnerving, were not fraudulent or patently threatening in such a way as to rise to the level of outrageous conduct. Plaintiff has not indicated that any amendment to the complaint would support a finding of outrageous conduct, so leave to amend the complaint is not appropriate here. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Defendant is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portions of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)
